The provider manual emphasizes mandatory Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, while policy documents primarily address immunization services for dual eligibles.", "policy_definition": "Pharmacies are only able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members, with dual eligible members continuing to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021]. This guidance was reiterated in August 2019, stating that dual-eligible enrollees will continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — August 01, 2019]. For general pharmacy claims, providers should refer to the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines – Pharmacy and the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]. Pharmacy providers must comply with NYS Medicaid policy for 340B drug claims as outlined in the NYRx the NY Medicaid Pharmacy Program – Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines and Medicaid Update articles [New York State Medicaid Update February 2024 Volume 40 Number 2:1 — February 01, 2024].", "provider_manual_definition": "All Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in accordance with federal and State laws and regulations [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full, and a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources acknowledge the role of Medicare for dual eligible members. The policy documents state that dual eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], and the provider manual mandates that Medicaid enrolled pharmacies submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services for dual eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:21, 22 — October 01, 2025]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly details the requirement for Medicare enrollment for pharmacies serving dual eligible members and outlines payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 21, 22 — October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents primarily focus on immunization services for dual eligible members, stating they access these services through Medicare, and direct providers to other billing guidelines for general pharmacy claims without detailing Medicare enrollment or payment specifics for dual eligibles [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — August 01, 2021], [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8:9 — April 01, 2023]."], "caveats": null}
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, emphasizing that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, reiterates Medicaid's role as the payer of last resort and the requirement to bill other liable third parties first.
answers.policy_definition: New York State Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment, which is managed through coordination of benefits (COB) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. Pharmacies are responsible for actively seeking Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients and for timely billing to the appropriate party, including commercial plans or Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would receive for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025]. Providers must determine if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other applicable third parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources highlight the pharmacy's responsibility to ensure proper billing to other insurance carriers before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that the combined payment must be accepted as payment in full, with no additional payment if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific instruction is not found in the provided policy documents.
comparison: Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other applicable third parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources highlight the pharmacy's responsibility to ensure proper billing to other insurance carriers before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and that the combined payment must be accepted as payment in full, with no additional payment if Medicare exceeds the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific instruction is not found in the provided policy documents.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and providers must bill other liable third parties first, including Medicare for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.

appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_7.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_8.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_8.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims, emphasizing that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The newest policy document, effective April 1, 2025, also states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other liable third parties first.
answers.policy_definition: New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. Pharmacies are responsible for actively and regularly seeking Third Party Liability (TPL) enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and for billing timely and to the appropriate party, such as a commercial plan or Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made, and the Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025]. Providers must determine if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both sources state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other applicable third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to identify and bill other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly details that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment for dual-eligible claims will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents, specifically the July 2020 update, mention the pharmacy's responsibility to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts regarding primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible claims.
comparison: Both sources state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill other applicable third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the provider's responsibility to identify and bill other insurance coverage [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly details that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment for dual-eligible claims will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and that Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full, prohibiting recovery of Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents, specifically the July 2020 update, mention the pharmacy's responsibility to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts regarding primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible claims.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_9.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_9.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_9.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_10.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_10.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document dated April 1, 2025, and should therefore receive more weight. Both sources agree that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and providers must bill other liable third parties, including Medicare, before billing Medicaid for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025]. Pharmacies are responsible for billing timely and to the appropriate party, such as a commercial plan or Medicare, before Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage, including Medicare, per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Any Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. Providers must determine if a patient is covered by other insurance and if that insurance covers the service being billed [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and that providers must bill other applicable third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility to identify and bill other insurance coverage, including Medicare, prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].
differences: The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and if Medicare payment is higher, no additional payment will be made, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual explicitly mentions that pharmacies should advise prescribers and members of potential delays and options for resolution if they encounter difficulties billing a third-party plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025], which is not detailed in the policy documents.
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025] and that providers must bill other applicable third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacy's responsibility to identify and bill other insurance coverage, including Medicare, prior to billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — July 01, 2020; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:18, 19 — July 23, 2025].

The provider manual specifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and if Medicare payment is higher, no additional payment will be made, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — October 01, 2025]. This specific detail is not present in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual explicitly mentions that pharmacies should advise prescribers and members of potential delays and options for resolution if they encounter difficulties billing a third-party plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16, 17 — October 01, 2025], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid program updated its Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025, emphasizing that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees are directed to "continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid generally "only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to these rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly prohibits pharmacy providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will only pay for certain drug classes excluded from Medicare Part D coverage for dual-eligible members, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and no additional payment is made if Medicare's payment is greater than the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific payment cap not mentioned in the policy documents.

Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-related co-payment responsibilities.
comparison: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly prohibits pharmacy providers from recovering Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will only pay for certain drug classes excluded from Medicare Part D coverage for dual-eligible members, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and no additional payment is made if Medicare's payment is greater than the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific payment cap not mentioned in the policy documents.

Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-related co-payment responsibilities.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, specifically stating that pharmacy providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance from Medicaid members. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, broadly states that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth, and Medicaid is the payer of last resort for general billing. Given the provider manual's later effective date and specific focus on pharmacy co-payments, it should receive more weight for current guidance on this topic.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2025, dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. For general billing, Medicaid is considered the payer of last resort, meaning other reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees continue to access them through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made. Both Medicare and Medicaid payments (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for services for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary or last-resort payer [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources acknowledge that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment for pharmacy services will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and if Medicare pays more, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail this specific payment cap or acceptance as payment in full.

Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and that duals are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Medicare Part D and Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for services for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as a secondary or last-resort payer [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources acknowledge that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacy providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual specifies that the combined Medicare/Medicaid payment for pharmacy services will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and if Medicare pays more, no additional Medicaid payment will be made, with both payments accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not detail this specific payment cap or acceptance as payment in full.

Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], and that duals are subject to Medicare rules for telehealth [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Medicare Part D and Part B [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
