test_id_12.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_12.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_12.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid program updated its Telehealth Policy Manual on July 1, 2025, emphasizing that "duals are subject to Medicare rules" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. Earlier policy guidance from February 1, 2023, states that "Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted" and that "potential third-party reimbursement sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid" [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees are directed to "continue to access immunization services through Medicare" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit "cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. It also states that "the total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient" and that "if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. For Medicare Part D members, Medicaid generally "only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans, such as: • Select prescription vitamins. and • Certain non-prescription drugs" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to these rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will only pay for certain excluded drug classes under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and no additional payment is made if Medicare's payment is greater than the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific payment cap not mentioned in the policy documents.

Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-related co-payment responsibilities.
comparison: Both sources indicate that Medicare is the primary payer for dual-eligible members, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules for certain services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid will only pay for certain excluded drug classes under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate, and no additional payment is made if Medicare's payment is greater than the Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific payment cap not mentioned in the policy documents.

Policy documents specifically mention that dual-eligible members access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-related co-payment responsibilities.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_14.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."

It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members, particularly regarding Medicare Part B and Part D, and should receive more weight. The newest policy document, effective July 1, 2025, primarily reiterates that dual-eligibles are subject to Medicare rules and that Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, effective July 1, 2025, dual-eligible members are subject to Medicare rules regarding telehealth services [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01]. Earlier policy guidance, effective February 1, 2023, states that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible enrollees, meaning Medicare and other third-party reimbursement sources must be billed first [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01]. For immunization services, dual-eligible enrollees continue to access them through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — 2019-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, a pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Additionally, for Medicare Part D members, Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to these rules [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that dual-eligible members are primarily covered by Medicare for certain services, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual provides specific guidance on when Medicaid will pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members (only for drug classes excluded by law from Part D plans) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific financial constraint not present in the policy excerpts.

Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — 2019-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Part B and Part D.
comparison: Both sources indicate that dual-eligible members are primarily covered by Medicare for certain services, with Medicaid acting as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7:2, 3 — 2025-07-01; Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1:10 — 2023-02-01; New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that providers cannot recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicaid members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a detail not found in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual provides specific guidance on when Medicaid will pay for drugs for Medicare Part D members (only for drug classes excluded by law from Part D plans) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only rate and must be accepted as payment in full [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01], a specific financial constraint not present in the policy excerpts.

Policy documents mention that dual-eligible members continue to access immunization services through Medicare [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:9 — 2021-08-01; New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:1 — 2019-08-01], while the provider manual focuses on pharmacy-specific billing for Part B and Part D.

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (May 1, 2023) and provides more specific guidance on Medicaid's role in covering Medicare Part D-excluded drugs for dual-eligible members, stating Medicaid will only pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. Policy documents, while acknowledging Medicare Part B covered drugs, do not explicitly detail Medicaid's coverage for Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy. The policy documents mention that when a member has Medicare, providers will bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. It also states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. However, there is no explicit definition of how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid will only pay for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member if the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans, and the drug is on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Examples of such excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. All claims for dual-eligible members must first be submitted to Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly defines Medicaid's role in covering Medicare Part D-excluded drugs for dual-eligible members, stating it will only pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents do not provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on Medicare Part B covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

The provider manual specifies that all claims for dual-eligible members must first be submitted to Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents state that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid, but do not specifically mention Part D in this context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
comparison: The provider manual explicitly defines Medicaid's role in covering Medicare Part D-excluded drugs for dual-eligible members, stating it will only pay for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents do not provide specific guidance on Medicaid's coverage of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on Medicare Part B covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

The provider manual specifies that all claims for dual-eligible members must first be submitted to Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The policy documents state that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid, but do not specifically mention Part D in this context [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].

policy.text:

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, provides more recent and specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs than the latest policy documents, which are dated May 1, 2023. The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid will only cover drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, for dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents discuss billing Medicare Part B for covered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — July 01, 2020] and practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — May 01, 2023], but do not specifically address Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Medicaid will only pay for prescription drugs for a Medicare Part D member if the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans. Examples of such excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members for these excluded drugs must be submitted to Medicaid. It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any other prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025]. The provided policy documents do not offer specific guidance on how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
comparison: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — October 01, 2025]. The provided policy documents do not offer specific guidance on how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs.
answers.caveats: The policy documents do not contain specific information regarding the handling of Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, making a direct comparison on this specific topic challenging. The provider manual, being more recent and specific, should be considered the primary guidance for this issue.

test_id_18.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document (May 1, 2023) and provides more specific guidance on Medicaid's role in covering Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, stating that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs that are specifically excluded by law from Part D plans. Policy documents, while older, generally indicate that if a primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, but also mention items not subject to the carve-out that should continue to be billed to the member's Medicare plan.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated May 1, 2023, New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy, with practitioner administered drugs (PADs) billed directly to NYRx listed on the New York State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01]. An older policy document from July 1, 2020, states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. Another policy document from March 1, 2021, notes that certain items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics, Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document are not subject to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s Medicare plan [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, it is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Medicaid only pays for these excluded drugs if they are on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, and examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. For dual-eligible members, all claims must first be submitted to Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount the pharmacy would have received for a Medicaid-only patient, and if the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee, no additional payment will be made [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply that Medicaid's coverage for drugs is secondary to Medicare, requiring claims to be submitted to Medicare first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to a list of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs for coverage [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, providing examples like select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents, while mentioning items not subject to carve-out that should be billed to Medicare, do not provide this specific legal exclusion criteria for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].

The provider manual details payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this level of detail regarding payment caps for Part D-excluded drugs.

An older policy document states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], which contrasts with the provider manual's explicit allowance for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs that are on the reimbursable list.
comparison: Both sources imply that Medicaid's coverage for drugs is secondary to Medicare, requiring claims to be submitted to Medicare first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to a list of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs for coverage [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D plans, providing examples like select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents, while mentioning items not subject to carve-out that should be billed to Medicare, do not provide this specific legal exclusion criteria for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out:5 — 2021-03-01].

The provider manual details payment limitations, stating that the total Medicare/Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee and no additional payment will be made if Medicare payment is greater [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents do not provide this level of detail regarding payment caps for Part D-excluded drugs.

An older policy document states that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], which contrasts with the provider manual's explicit allowance for Medicaid coverage of Part D-excluded drugs that are on the reimbursable list.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.text:
Clarification Regarding Pharmacy and Practitioner Dispensing of
Drugs Requiring Administration by a Practitioner
New York State (NYS) Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs require administration by a practitioner and
may be billed to NYS Medicaid by either a practitioner or a pharmacy. Practitioner administered drugs (PADs)
billed directly to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, by a pharmacy, are listed on the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located on the eMedNY “Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs” web page at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx. The intent of
this article is to provide guidance for proper dispensing and delivery of such drugs. Nothing in this policy is
meant to suggest that any or all PADs must be dispensed by a pharmacy.
PADs Billed by a Practitioner
PADs billed by a practitioner using medical claims format should refer to the policy and guidance regarding
PAD billing located on the eMedNY “Physician Manual” web page, at: https://www.emedny.org/Provider
Manuals/Physician/index.aspx.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_18.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

answers.headline_summary: The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual (effective 2025-10-01) provides more recent and specific guidance on Medicare Part D-excluded drugs than the New York State Medicaid Update (newest relevant policy effective 2023-05-01). The provider manual clarifies that Medicaid only covers drugs specifically excluded by law from Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs, and should receive more weight for current guidance.

Examples of such excluded drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. It is not appropriate to bill Medicaid for other prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid only pays for drugs for Medicare Part D members if the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Part D coverage, providing examples like select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:18, 19 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the provided policy documents do not offer specific guidance on how Medicaid handles Medicare Part D-excluded drugs, focusing instead on Medicare Part B covered drugs and practitioner-administered drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10:11 — 2023-05-01].

• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
