00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both indicate that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" of the third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific clarification not found in the provided policy documents regarding prior authorization after Medicaid payment for crossover claims.

An earlier policy document from July 2020 states that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], while the newest provider manual focuses on the responsibility of the third party even if prior authorization is not obtained.
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both indicate that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" of the third party to pay first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific clarification not found in the provided policy documents regarding prior authorization after Medicaid payment for crossover claims.

An earlier policy document from July 2020 states that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], while the newest provider manual focuses on the responsibility of the third party even if prior authorization is not obtained.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_27.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_27.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_27.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization from a third party does not negate the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid. The newest policy documents, effective November 1, 2021, also emphasize that prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party before submitting a claim to Medicaid. Given the provider manual's more recent effective date, it should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: For crossover claims, prior authorization requirements must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment, and providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01]
answers.provider_manual_definition: Federal regulations mandate that all available resources be utilized before Medicaid considers payment. Providers are required to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome this responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also notes that prior authorization and editing on prescriptions for all topical compounded drug products is required for certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — 2025-10-01]. When billing, if a prescription requires prior approval or authorization, the number must be entered in Field 12. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23]
similarities: Both sources state that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that failure to obtain prior authorization from the third party does not overcome the responsibility to pursue other coverage. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. While the policy documents imply this by stating that prior authorization issues must be resolved with the third party, they do not use the exact phrasing regarding 'overcoming responsibility'. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]

The policy documents state that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit to the third party will result in denial. [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. The provider manual does not explicitly state these consequences in the provided chunks, though it emphasizes the primary payor responsibility. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]
comparison: Both sources state that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

Both sources require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]

[New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]

Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

test_id_28.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."

Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.

This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, indicates that failure to obtain prior authorization from a third party does not negate the responsibility to pursue other coverage before billing Medicaid. The newest policy document, effective November 1, 2021, also emphasizes the requirement to pursue other coverage and resolve prior authorization issues with third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid. Given the provider manual's more recent effective date, it should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the "New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13" effective November 1, 2021, federal regulations mandate that all available resources be utilized before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If a responsible third party should be paying as the primary payor, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01]. An earlier policy document, "New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12" effective July 1, 2020, states that any claim issues, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, must be resolved with the third party prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. It also notes that if the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim, and failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, the "New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines" effective October 1, 2025, federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate that a provider pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Crucially, failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome this responsibility [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also mentions that prior authorization and editing on prescriptions for all topical compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — 2025-10-01]. The "New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines" effective July 23, 2025, instructs providers to enter the prior approval/authorization number if billing for a prescription/order that requires it [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines:15, 16 — 2025-07-23].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific clarification not found in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents, specifically the July 2020 Medicaid Update, state that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. While the provider manual implies this through the 'failure to obtain PA' clause, the policy document is more direct about the requirement to resolve PA issues with the third party first.
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that if a responsible third party is the primary payor, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a specific clarification not found in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents, specifically the July 2020 Medicaid Update, state that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. While the provider manual implies this through the 'failure to obtain PA' clause, the policy document is more direct about the requirement to resolve PA issues with the third party first.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
November 2021 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 15

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service
(FFS) claims. This process will ensure that compounded topical drug products meet State and Federal

regulations and that the compound ingredients are Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved or
compendia-supported for topical use when submitted to NYRx.
Pharmacies that submit compounds may receive the following edits:
Edit# Edit Description NCPDP Reject Reject Message Notes/Resolutions
Code
Item Not Eligible For Product not on
00551 MR
Payment On Fill Date formulary
PA Required Call Prime
Therapeutics State
Government Solutions. This is
specific to topical compounds.
To obtain a PA, the prescriber
must call the prior
authorization clinical call
Compound Claim Prior Authorization center at 1-877-309-9493.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Both sources state that Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that if a responsible third party exists, they must pay first [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13:15, 16 — 2021-11-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that "Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility" to pursue other coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:16 — 2025-10-01], a direct statement not found in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents explicitly state that "Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid" and that "Failure to submit the claim to the third party will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid" [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:7 — 2020-07-01]. While the provider manual implies this through the responsibility to pursue other coverage, it does not contain this exact phrasing regarding resolution of PA issues with the third party or the direct consequence of denial for failure to submit to the third party.

The provider manual details specific requirements for prior authorization on topical compounded drug products for NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:36, 37 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the provided policy excerpts.

policy.text:
Notification to Providers of Requirement to Attach Explanation of
Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims
To ensure that New York State (NYS) Medicaid is the payor of last resort when a Medicaid recipient is eligible
for Medicare or has other third-party insurance benefits, providers must first bill Medicare or the other third-
party insurance for services prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. In the coming months, providers will be
required to attach an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) for fee-for-service (FFS) claims when submitting a claim
to Medicaid for services rendered to a recipient who is also eligible for Medicare or has other third-party
coverage. If an EOB is not submitted with the claim, the claim will be denied. The Office of the Medicaid
Inspector General (OMIG) will be providing additional information regarding the timeline and requirements for
implementation of this initiative.
Please Note: Pharmacy claims, Medicare crossover claims, and non-covered procedure codes identified by
NYS Medicaid are not subject to this requirement.
Questions regarding this notice should be directed to OMIG’s Bureau of Third Party and Payment Oversight
at ThirdP@omig.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2020 Volume 36 Number 15
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients
with Other Coverage
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to
submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid
member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. As previously
communicated in the October 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled Notification to Providers
of Requirement to Attach Explanation of Benefits from Third-Party Payors to Medicaid Claims, available at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no15_oct20.pdf, failure to
obtain prior authorization does not overcome the responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will deny at point of service with National Council for Prescription
Drug Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”.

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

Prior Authorization
• Per the September 2015 NYS Medicaid Drug Utilization Review (DUR) Board recommendation and the
November 2018 Medicaid Update article titled, Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service Prior Authorization of
Topical Compounded Drug Products, Prior Authorization (PA) and editing on prescriptions for all topical
compounded drug products is required and will be implemented on certain NYRX Medicaid Fee-for-Service

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 12)
If the provider is billing for a prescription/order that requires prior approval or prior authorization, enter the prior
approval/authorization number assigned for the prescription/order.
Line (Field 12A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 12 applies. If the prior
approval/authorization number entered in field 12 applies to all claim lines, enter an “A”.
Prior Approval/Authorization No. (Field 13)
If a prior approval/authorization number different from the one entered in field 12 applies to another claim line in the
same claim form, enter the other prior approval/authorization number.

Line (Field 13A)
Enter the claim line number to which the prior approval/authorization entered in field 13 applies.
NOTES:
For information regarding how to obtain Prior Approval/Prior Authorization for specific services, please refer
to the Information for All Providers, Inquiry section on the web page for this manual.

test_id_30.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_30.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_30.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_31.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on Coordination of Benefits (COB) requirements. Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill applicable third parties first.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. This process is managed through Coordination of Benefits (COB) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. COB ensures the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For Workers’ Compensation cases, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if the drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer by using an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual imply that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other liable parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents provide a general requirement for COB, stating that providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. In contrast, the provider manual specifically details COB requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases, outlining when Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as the primary payer for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual imply that NYS Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other liable parties to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents provide a general requirement for COB, stating that providers must bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. In contrast, the provider manual specifically details COB requirements for Workers’ Compensation cases, outlining when Workers’ Compensation coverage must be utilized and when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as the primary payer for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
answers.caveats: The provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy-related COB requirements, particularly concerning Workers' Compensation, while the policy documents provide a broader, more general definition of COB for all Medicaid providers. The provider manual does not explicitly state the federal regulation requirement mentioned in the policy documents.

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

test_id_31.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_32.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB) related to Workers' Compensation, while the newest policy documents, effective April 1, 2025, reiterate the general requirement for providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. This ensures that the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For members with a work-related injury and an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. If a drug claim is not related to a member's Workers' Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer by using an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources emphasize that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring other available resources to be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].
differences: The provider manual provides specific instructions for COB in cases of Workers' Compensation, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as primary for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents provide a general requirement for providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid without specific examples or codes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].
comparison: Both sources emphasize that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring other available resources to be used first [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].

The provider manual provides specific instructions for COB in cases of Workers' Compensation, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx Medicaid can be billed as primary for unrelated conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. In contrast, the policy documents provide a general requirement for providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid without specific examples or codes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01].

policy.text:
Additional information regarding Coordination
of Benefits (COB) can be found in the following Medicaid Update articles:
• Update on Coordination of Benefits Submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing article
published in the October 2022 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://
www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/docs/mu_no12_oct22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Medicaid Requires Coordination of Benefits article published in the April 2022 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2022/
docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/
health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_nov21_pr.pdf; and
• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer
ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier article published in the October 2017 issue of the Medicaid Update,
located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/oct17_mu.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
