answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, which has an effective date of April 1, 2025. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance on Coordination of Benefits (COB) requirements. Both sources agree that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill applicable third parties first.
answers.policy_definition: New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment. This process is managed through Coordination of Benefits (COB) [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01]. COB ensures the correct party pays first, and if there is a responsible third-party such as a health insurance provider, they should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. If the drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources require that other available resources or applicable third parties be utilized before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents provide a general requirement for COB across all Medicaid services [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], while the provider manual specifically details COB requirements in the context of Workers’ Compensation for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual introduces the concept of an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 for situations where NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for non-work-related conditions, which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual introduces the concept of an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 for situations where NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for non-work-related conditions, which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].

COB ensures the correct party pays first, and if there is a responsible third-party such as a health insurance provider, they should pay first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — 2022-10-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "For Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits, if a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]. If the drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both sources indicate that NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "Both sources require that other available resources or applicable third parties be utilized before billing NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01] and [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The policy documents provide a general requirement for COB across all Medicaid services [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — 2025-04-01], while the provider manual specifically details COB requirements in the context of Workers’ Compensation for pharmacy claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01].", "The provider manual introduces the concept of an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 for situations where NYRx may be billed as the primary payer for non-work-related conditions, which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — 2025-10-01]."], "caveats": null}
test_id_33.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

test_id_34.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific guidance on Coordination of Benefits (COB) for Workers' Compensation cases, while the newest policy documents, effective April 1, 2025, reiterate the general requirement for providers to bill third parties before NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and offers more specific instructions for certain COB scenarios.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid, as NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be utilized before NYS Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]. This ensures that the correct party pays first [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — October 1, 2022]. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — October 1, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For members with a work-related injury and an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. If a drug claim is not related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both policy and provider manual documents implicitly agree that other available resources, such as third-party payers or Workers' Compensation, must be utilized before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific instructions for Coordination of Benefits in cases involving Workers’ Compensation, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as primary with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. The newest policy documents, effective April 1, 2025, provide a general requirement for providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid without detailing specific scenarios like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025].
comparison: Both policy and provider manual documents implicitly agree that other available resources, such as third-party payers or Workers' Compensation, must be utilized before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].

The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific instructions for Coordination of Benefits in cases involving Workers’ Compensation, detailing when Workers' Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx may be billed as primary with a specific Eligibility Clarification Code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. The newest policy documents, effective April 1, 2025, provide a general requirement for providers to bill applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid without detailing specific scenarios like Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025].

docs/mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf;
• Reminder: Pharmacy Billing for Medicaid Patients with Other Coverage article published in the
November 2021 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/

• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note:
• Outpatient dispensing, community-based, open-door pharmacies applying for enrollment in COS 0441
must be full service. This is defined as dispensing and maintaining an inventory of commonly dispensed
outpatient prescription medications, as well as providing diabetic supplies and immunizations. Community
pharmacies that serve a particular diagnosis, situation, or niche or that do not immunize do not meet COS
0441 enrollment criteria.
• Pharmacies that are embedded or located (not separate and distinct) within a healthcare facility, physician’s
office, or group, or in a hospital setting, are not classified or enrollable under COS 0441 (see clinic and
hospital pharmacy COS below), this includes pharmacies with pass through windows or doors, or situated
where patients have to walk through a healthcare facility, office area, or otherwise to reach the pharmacy or
those that share a waiting area.
• Pharmacies whose physical registered space is not separate and distinct from other unaffiliated or
separately operated retail or medical entities do not qualify for pharmacy enrollment.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of the Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee [New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12:6, 7 — October 1, 2022].", "provider_manual_definition": "For members with a work-related injury and an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers’ Compensation case (e.g., a maintenance medication for an unrelated chronic condition), NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].", "similarities": ["Both sources implicitly agree that other payers should be billed before Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort, although the provider manual focuses specifically on Workers' Compensation [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025]; [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025]."], "differences": ["The newest policy document (April 1, 2025) provides a general requirement for providers to bill all applicable third parties before NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4:7, 8 — April 1, 2025], while the newest provider manual (October 1, 2025) offers specific instructions for Workers’ Compensation cases, detailing when Workers’ Compensation must be utilized and when NYRx can be billed as primary [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025].", "The provider manual introduces the use of an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9 for claims unrelated to a Workers’ Compensation case where NYRx is the primary payer [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:17 — October 1, 2025], which is not mentioned in the provided policy documents."], "caveats": null}
test_id_35.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_36.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, and should receive more weight by default. It states that pharmacists are not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available or the prescription is legitimate, and they must make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and prescriber's identity. The newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, focus on contacting the PCP for emergency prescriptions and consulting the prescriber for prescription directions.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, effective January 1, 2025, if prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit and the assigned PCP is unavailable, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment in filling them without regard to restriction requirements and for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Additionally, providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source, effective October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items on the prescription if the information is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly addresses missing patient information such as address, age, or CIN, stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not directly address these specific missing patient data points.

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents mention consulting the prescriber for directions [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01] and notifying the prescriber for prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], but not the initial verification of an unknown prescriber's identity.

Policy documents outline responsibilities for emergency prescriptions, including using professional judgment and contacting the PCP the next business day if the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], which is not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.
comparison: The provider manual explicitly addresses missing patient information such as address, age, or CIN, stating pharmacists are not required to enter it if readily available or the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not directly address these specific missing patient data points.

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01]. Policy documents mention consulting the prescriber for directions [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01] and notifying the prescriber for prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], but not the initial verification of an unknown prescriber's identity.

Policy documents outline responsibilities for emergency prescriptions, including using professional judgment and contacting the PCP the next business day if the PCP is unavailable [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], which is not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_36.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

test_id_37.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_38.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_38.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on pharmacists' responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, and should receive more weight by default. The newest policy document, effective January 1, 2025, focuses on emergency situations and general responsibilities.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, in emergency department visit scenarios where the assigned PCP is unavailable, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment to fill prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements. The pharmacy must then contact the assigned PCP the next business day to verify these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Additionally, providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber when presented with such a prescription [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information on the prescription if it is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions, either by verifying information or contacting prescribers [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly addresses missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) and provides conditions under which a pharmacist is not required to enter it on the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not detail this specific scenario.

Policy documents focus on responsibilities related to emergency department prescriptions and contacting PCPs the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01] and notifying prescribers for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], which are not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber's identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], a detail not explicitly covered in the policy excerpts regarding missing information.
comparison: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions, either by verifying information or contacting prescribers [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly addresses missing patient demographic information (address, age, CIN) and provides conditions under which a pharmacist is not required to enter it on the prescription [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not detail this specific scenario.

Policy documents focus on responsibilities related to emergency department prescriptions and contacting PCPs the next business day [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01] and notifying prescribers for non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01], which are not detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber's identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], a detail not explicitly covered in the policy excerpts regarding missing information.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_39.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_40.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_40.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on pharmacist responsibilities when patient or prescriber information is missing, and should receive more weight by default. It clarifies that pharmacists are not required to enter missing address, age, or CIN if the information is readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, and emphasizes verifying prescriber identity. The newest policy documents, effective January 1, 2025, focus on contacting PCPs for emergency prescriptions and consulting prescribers for prescription directions.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, effective January 1, 2025, the pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgment in filling emergency prescriptions without regard to restriction requirements if the assigned PCP is unavailable, and must contact the assigned PCP the next business day regarding these prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01]. Additionally, providers should verify prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01]. For non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization, the pharmacist must notify the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, if the address, age, or CIN of a Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information if it is readily available in the pharmacy's records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01]. The pharmacist is also responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions, either by verifying information or consulting with prescribers [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient information (address, age, CIN) if it's readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents focus on contacting prescribers for clarification on directions or prior authorization for non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

Policy documents outline responsibilities related to emergency prescriptions, requiring pharmacists to use professional judgment and contact PCPs the next business day if unavailable, a detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01].
comparison: Both sources imply a responsibility for pharmacists to ensure the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions, either by verifying information or consulting with prescribers [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that pharmacists are not required to enter missing patient information (address, age, CIN) if it's readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, which is not directly addressed in the policy documents [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:4, 5 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual specifies the pharmacist's responsibility to make a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if unknown [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:27, 28 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents focus on contacting prescribers for clarification on directions or prior authorization for non-preferred drugs [New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10:1 — 2024-09-01], [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12:11 — 2023-07-01].

Policy documents outline responsibilities related to emergency prescriptions, requiring pharmacists to use professional judgment and contact PCPs the next business day if unavailable, a detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:7 — 2025-01-01].

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
The pharmacy is responsible for using professional judgement in filling prescriptions without
regard to restriction requirements if the prescriptions were written as part of an emergency department visit
and the assigned PCP is unavailable (i.e., after hours) to verify the prescriptions. The pharmacy is responsible
for contacting the assigned PCP the next business day regarding the prescriptions obtained by the recipient.
An assigned primary inpatient hospital is responsible for providing all non-emergency inpatient services to the
restricted recipient except for services provided pursuant to an authorized referral.
Emergency Pharmacy Situations
In the event of an emergency, such as drug shortages, unexpected pharmacy closures, and/or unexpected
travel, providers and pharmacies must refer to the instructions and contact information provided below.
During normal business hours, the restricted recipient can obtain a temporary authorization to fill the
prescription at an alternate pharmacy location by contacting:
• the New York State (NYS) Office of the Medicaid Inspector General (OMIG)
Call (518) 474-6866 or email omig.sm.rrp@omig.ny.gov (available Monday through Friday
from 8 a.m.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. The New York State Medicaid Update from April 2024 states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title 19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. This exclusion is also noted in the April 2022 Medicaid Update, which specifies "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)]" are excluded for both FFS and MMC programs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The January 2025 Medicaid Update lists "drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid" as examples of drugs excluded from State Plan Coverage [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under the New York State Medicaid Program. The Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Socia [...]" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. It further clarifies that "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, [...]" are examples of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for exclusion of certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The policy documents, specifically the April 2022 Medicaid Update, also mention "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" as excluded, and cite NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) in addition to federal regulations [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The provider manual mentions "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" but does not explicitly include "weight gain" in the same context [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for exclusion of certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

The policy documents, specifically the April 2022 Medicaid Update, also mention "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" as excluded, and cite NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) in addition to federal regulations [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The provider manual mentions "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia" but does not explicitly include "weight gain" in the same context [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
