test_id_41.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."

test_id_42.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_42.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: Weight loss drugs are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The newest Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides the most current guidance, reinforcing exclusions also stated in the newest Policy Update effective January 1, 2025.
answers.policy_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Update effective January 1, 2025, drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid. This is in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation, and such drugs are examples of those not reimbursable. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 1, 2025]. Earlier policy updates, such as the one effective April 1, 2024, also state that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024]. Similarly, an update from April 1, 2022, specifies that agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are exclusions for both FFS and MMC program areas, pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3). [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 1, 2022].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, effective October 1, 2025, drugs used for the treatment of anorexia or weight loss are explicitly listed as examples of drugs/drug uses that are not reimbursable by Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025]. Furthermore, the manual states that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both the newest policy and provider manual documents explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for excluding drugs for certain indications, including weight loss, particularly in the context of compounded medications. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The newest Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest Policy Update, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guiding document. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 1, 2025].

The policy documents, specifically the April 2022 update, additionally cite New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as a basis for excluding agents used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain for both FFS and MMC program areas, which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 1, 2022].
comparison: Both the newest policy and provider manual documents explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid. [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as a basis for excluding drugs for certain indications, including weight loss, particularly in the context of compounded medications. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — April 1, 2024], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — October 1, 2025].

The newest Provider Manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest Policy Update, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guiding document. [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — October 1, 2025], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — January 1, 2025].

The policy documents, specifically the April 2022 update, additionally cite New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3) as a basis for excluding agents used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain for both FFS and MMC program areas, which is not explicitly mentioned in the provided provider manual excerpts. [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — April 1, 2022].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

The policy documents specifically mention the exclusion for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], while the provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly differentiate between these program areas for this exclusion.
comparison: Both sources explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reference the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding certain indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention that compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications like weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy update, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].

The policy documents specifically mention the exclusion for both FFS and MMC program areas [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01], while the provided provider manual excerpts do not explicitly differentiate between these program areas for this exclusion.

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, and the newest policy source dated January 1, 2025, drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The provider manual is more recent and should receive more weight by default.
answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. This exclusion is noted in the context of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid, and also specifically for agents used for the treatment of weight loss. Additionally, compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including drugs to treat weight loss, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). answers.policy_definition: NYS Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for weight loss. The New York State Medicaid Update from April 2024 states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)... including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes." [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01]. This is reiterated in the April 2022 update, which lists "agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" as exclusions for both FFS and MMC programs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4:1 — 2022-04-01]. The January 2025 update also notes that "The following are examples of drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance" with state plan coverage [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: Drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid. The New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly states that "Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01]. It further clarifies that "Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain" are examples of drugs/drug uses not reimbursable by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:28 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reference Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention that compounds may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].
comparison: Both the policy documents and the provider manual explicitly state that drugs used for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources reference Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention that compounds may not be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4:6, 7 — 2024-04-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent than the newest policy document, effective January 1, 2025, making it the most current guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:34 — 2025-10-01], [New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1:13 — 2025-01-01].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
*Requires additional MEVS.
Denial code “722”: NDC DESI code is invalid.
NDC Excluded from State Plan Coverage
NYS Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State Department of Health
List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx (unless provided
by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by NYS Medicaid in accordance with policy and/or state or federal
legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2),
located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm, and SOS §365-a(4)(f), located at: https://www.
nysenate.gov/legislation/laws/SOS/365-A.
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), located at: https://
www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2025 Volume 41 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_45.ground_truth:
"The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.

The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications."
test_id_45.pm_ground_truth: The manual provides no specific examples of modern weight loss drugs (like GLP-1 agonists). It relies entirely on the general categorical exclusion, which could be open to interpretation with newer medications.
test_id_45.policy_ground_truth: The examples given are: Wegovy, Ozempic, Qsymia, and Mounjaro. This is the most significant clarification, as it removes all ambiguity regarding these popular medications.

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides a more detailed and current explanation of 340B drug reimbursement, stating it is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost, while the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2022, focuses on identification requirements for 340B drugs. The provider manual should receive more weight as it is significantly more recent.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the "New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8" effective July 1, 2022, 340B drugs must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01]. Earlier policy documents, such as the "New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9" effective August 1, 2019, state that 340B drug claims are required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care (MMC) members and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01]. If the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, the claim will be denied [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - 2019-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the "New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines" effective October 1, 2025, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01]. When submitting 340B drug claims in the NCPDP format, specific fields are required, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, and the Ingredient Cost Submitted as the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01]. The 340B ceiling price is defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate percentage, also known as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be properly identified when submitted for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the 340B acquisition cost as a key factor in reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to a 'ceiling price' or maximum allowed amount for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01].
differences: The newest policy document specifies appending the UD modifier for FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01], while the newest provider manual details specific NCPDP fields (SCC 20, BCD 08) for claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit at this cost and the consequences of not doing so [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01].

The provider manual provides a definition of the 340B Ceiling Price, linking it to AMP and URA [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01], which is not explicitly defined in the provided policy documents.
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be properly identified when submitted for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the 340B acquisition cost as a key factor in reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01].

Both sources refer to a 'ceiling price' or maximum allowed amount for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01].

The newest policy document specifies appending the UD modifier for FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - 2022-07-01], while the newest provider manual details specific NCPDP fields (SCC 20, BCD 08) for claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit at this cost and the consequences of not doing so [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - 2019-08-01].

The provider manual provides a definition of the 340B Ceiling Price, linking it to AMP and URA [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 - 2025-10-01], which is not explicitly defined in the provided policy documents.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:

Field

to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more detailed guidance on 340B drug reimbursement, stating that claims are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost, while the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2022, focuses on identification requirements for 340B drugs on claims. The provider manual should receive more weight by default due to its later effective date.
answers.policy_definition: For 340B drugs, providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) and identify the drugs by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims; failure to reference the NDC will result in no reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01]. Previously, 340B drug claims submitted via the NCPDP D.0 format were required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care (MMC) members, and submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — 2019-08-01]. Claims with an ingredient cost higher than the ceiling price would be denied with specific denial codes [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 — 2019-08-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01]. When submitting Medicaid 340B drug claims in the NCPDP format, specific fields are required, including Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, and the Ingredient Cost Submitted as the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01]. The 340B ceiling price is defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the Unit Rebate Amount (URA) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01]. Providers must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for their Medicaid patients upon enrollment in the 340B program, and federal law prohibits duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be identified as such on claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the 340B acquisition cost as a key factor in reimbursement or submission [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit claims at this cost and the consequences of not doing so [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — 2019-08-01].

The provider manual details specific NCPDP fields required for 340B drug claims, such as SCC 20 and BCD 08 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents.

The policy document from July 2022 specifies appending the UD modifier for 340B drugs on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], a detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts.
The provider manual defines the 340B Ceiling Price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01], which is not defined in the provided policy documents.
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be identified as such on claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources mention the 340B acquisition cost as a key factor in reimbursement or submission [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — 2019-08-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit claims at this cost and the consequences of not doing so [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 — 2019-08-01].

The provider manual details specific NCPDP fields required for 340B drug claims, such as SCC 20 and BCD 08 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 — 2025-10-01], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided policy documents.

The policy document from July 2022 specifies appending the UD modifier for 340B drugs on FFS-only claims [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 — 2022-07-01], a detail not found in the provided provider manual excerpts.
The provider manual defines the 340B Ceiling Price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:44 — 2025-10-01], which is not defined in the provided policy documents.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) * Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash
to Cash Customers Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9

policy.text:
Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.

for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: applying claim level identifiers on non-340B purchased drug claims is
considered fraudulent billing.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format:
Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field Medicaid Primary Claim (Medicare. Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.

test_id_48.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_48.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

test_id_49.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides a more detailed and current explanation of 340B drug reimbursement, stating it is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost, while the newest policy document, effective July 1, 2022, focuses on identification requirements for 340B drugs. The provider manual should receive more weight as it is significantly more recent.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the "New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8" effective July 1, 2022, 340B drugs must be identified by appending the "UD modifier to the 340B drug line on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims" to receive reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - July 1, 2022]. Earlier policy documents, such as the "New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9" effective August 1, 2019, state that 340B drug claims for fee-for-service (FFS) members must be "submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider" [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - August 1, 2019]. If the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, the claim will be denied with specific denial codes [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - August 1, 2019].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the "New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines" effective October 1, 2025, 340B drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - October 1, 2025]. This manual also specifies that "340B Purchased" drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" with a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - October 1, 2025]. For Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted in the NCPDP format, specific fields are required, including "409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted" which must be the "340B Acquisition Cost" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025]. The manual also notes that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025].
similarities: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be identified as such for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - July 1, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025].

Both sources mention the importance of submitting the correct 340B acquisition cost for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:5 - August 1, 2019], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" and includes a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - October 1, 2025], while the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit the acquisition cost and identification modifiers without detailing the exact reimbursement methodology or dispensing fee in the newest policy update [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - July 1, 2022].

The provider manual details specific NCPDP fields required for 340B drug claims, such as "420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20" and "423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08" [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025], which are not explicitly mentioned in the provided policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts" for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025], a detail not present in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents mention that claims with ingredient costs higher than the ceiling price will be denied with specific denial codes [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - August 1, 2019], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.
comparison: Both sources indicate that 340B drugs must be identified as such for reimbursement [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - July 1, 2022], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025].

The provider manual explicitly states that 340B drugs are reimbursed at the "340B Acquisition Cost" and includes a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:32 - October 1, 2025], while the policy documents primarily focus on the requirement to submit the acquisition cost and identification modifiers without detailing the exact reimbursement methodology or dispensing fee in the newest policy update [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:5 - July 1, 2022].

The provider manual clarifies that "Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts" for 340B drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:38 - October 1, 2025], a detail not present in the provided policy documents.

The policy documents mention that claims with ingredient costs higher than the ceiling price will be denied with specific denial codes [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9:6 - August 1, 2019], which is not explicitly detailed in the provided provider manual excerpts.

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.

Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies

indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to

Any pharmacy that submits a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling
price, will be returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code “708: Exceeds NY
Allowed Maximum” and the NCPDP Reject code "23: M/I Ingredient Cost.” The pharmacy would then have
to resubmit the claim with the correct ingredient cost.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

considered fraudulent billing.

Medicaid Secondary Claim

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 N/A
409-D9 Ingredient Cost Submitted 340B Acquisition Cost N/A
Lowest Net Charge to Cash Lowest Net Charge to Cash
426-DQ Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) Customers Customers
FAQs on the 340B program itself, as well as information on how to ask additional questions, can be found on the HRSA
website at: https://www.hrsa.gov/opa/faqs/index.html.
