test_id_51.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_51.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment and drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, broadly cover drugs administered by various methods but do not specifically address home infusion billing responsibilities, making the provider manual the more current and relevant source for this specific inquiry.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source dated July 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in compendia, or under an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. An older policy document from July 1, 2020, states that it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a healthcare provider, can be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered by intravenous methods [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies are involved in the billing process for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The newest provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not mention bundled payments for home infusion.

The newest provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' as a distinct category of service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents discuss drugs administered by various methods (subcutaneous, intramuscular, intravenous) more generally without specific focus on 'home infusion' as a service type [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual details criteria for drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents for home infusion drugs.
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered by intravenous methods [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies are involved in the billing process for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The newest provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not mention bundled payments for home infusion.

The newest provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' as a distinct category of service [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents discuss drugs administered by various methods (subcutaneous, intramuscular, intravenous) more generally without specific focus on 'home infusion' as a service type [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual details criteria for drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not detailed in the policy documents for home infusion drugs.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provide general guidance on drug coverage and billing responsibilities for pharmacies, but the provider manual offers more specific and detailed information regarding home infusion drugs and their billing mechanisms. Given the provider manual's later effective date (October 1, 2025), its guidance on home infusion should be considered more current and authoritative for this specific topic.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider can be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit, and are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Prescription drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge that drugs administered by intravenous methods are covered by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not mention bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' and the billing mechanisms for these drugs and supplies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents discuss coverage for drugs administered by various methods (subcutaneous, intramuscular, intravenous) in a practitioner's office without specific reference to 'home infusion' as a distinct service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual details criteria for drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not specified in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that prescription drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents broadly state it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
comparison: Both sources acknowledge that drugs administered by intravenous methods are covered by Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas policy documents do not mention bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' and the billing mechanisms for these drugs and supplies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents discuss coverage for drugs administered by various methods (subcutaneous, intramuscular, intravenous) in a practitioner's office without specific reference to 'home infusion' as a distinct service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

The provider manual details criteria for drugs to be eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit, such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not specified in the policy documents.

The provider manual clarifies that prescription drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01], while policy documents broadly state it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill the appropriate party [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_52.ground_truth:
The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_52.policy_ground_truth: The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_53.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_53.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, primarily address general drug coverage for various administration methods and pharmacy billing responsibilities, but do not specifically detail home infusion billing. Given its later effective date, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2022, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office are covered for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in compendia, or under an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. An earlier policy from February 2019 also states that drugs administered by these methods are covered for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. The pharmacy is responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].

NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a healthcare provider, need to be available via both medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Prescription drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources indicate that pharmacies have billing responsibilities for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:30, 31 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not address bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifically details that home infusion drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria like being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], a level of detail not present in the policy documents regarding home infusion.

test_id_54.question: How are home infusion drugs covered, and who is responsible for billing
test_id_54.concept: home infusion drugs coverage and billing responsibilities

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, primarily address general drug coverage for various administration methods and pharmacy billing responsibilities, but lack specific details on home infusion billing. Given the provider manual's later effective date, it should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: According to the newest policy source, the New York State Medicaid Update July 2022, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office are covered for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in compendia described in Social Security Act §1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. The New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 states that it is the pharmacy's responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].
answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, the New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, the program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, and home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. NYS Medicaid recognizes that certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a healthcare provider, need to be available via both medical and pharmacy benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format and being able to be dispensed by a pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].
similarities: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered by intravenous methods [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not address bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifically details that home infusion drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria like being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the policy documents.

The policy documents discuss general pharmacy billing responsibilities to various parties (facility, commercial plan, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual focuses on how home infusion drugs and supplies should be billed (pharmacy or medical benefit) without detailing the specific parties responsible for billing.
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered by intravenous methods [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].

Both sources imply that pharmacies have a role in billing for drugs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01].

The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments, requiring drugs and supplies to be billed separately as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not address bundled payments for home infusion.

The provider manual specifically details that home infusion drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria like being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01], which is not mentioned in the policy documents.

The policy documents discuss general pharmacy billing responsibilities to various parties (facility, commercial plan, Medicare, Medicaid) [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01], while the provider manual focuses on how home infusion drugs and supplies should be billed (pharmacy or medical benefit) without detailing the specific parties responsible for billing.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
NYS Medicaid FFS policy for drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in
the practitioner’s office are covered for Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved indications, or where
the medically accepted indication is supported in any of the compendia described in Social Security Act
§1927(g)(1)(B)(i) for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service. In the absence of such a
recognized indication, an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol would be required with
documentation maintained in the patient's clinical file. Drugs are not covered for investigational or
experimental use.
The NYS Medicaid program has also issued policies and billing guidance for certain drugs/drug classes.
These drugs are eligible for reimbursement when the clinical criteria outlined on the NYS Department of
Health (DOH) New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Practitioner Administered Drug Policies and Billing
Guidance web page, located at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/practitioner_
administered/ffs_practitioner_administer.htm, and listed web page Medicaid Update articles, are met.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment and drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, broadly cover drugs administered by various methods but do not specifically address home infusion billing responsibilities, making the provider manual the more current and relevant source for this specific inquiry.

An older policy from February 1, 2019, also states that drugs administered by these methods are covered for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. Regarding billing responsibility, a policy from July 1, 2020, indicates it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].

Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. The provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' as a distinct category of treatment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents discuss drugs administered by intravenous methods more generally without specific reference to 'home infusion' billing or coverage details.

The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent and provides more detailed, specific guidance regarding home infusion drug coverage and billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] compared to the policy documents, with the newest relevant policy effective July 1, 2022 [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01].

Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_55.answer: {"headline_summary": "The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing, stating that the NYS Medicaid Program does not offer a bundled payment and drugs/supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The newest policy documents, effective July 1, 2022, broadly cover drugs administered by various methods but do not specifically address home infusion billing responsibilities, making the provider manual the more current and relevant source for this specific inquiry.", "policy_definition": "According to the newest policy source dated July 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid FFS policy covers drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods in a practitioner’s office for FDA-approved indications or medically accepted indications supported in compendia, or under an approved Institutional Review Board (IRB) protocol for a medically necessary NYS Medicaid-covered service [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]. An older policy from February 1, 2019, also states that drugs administered by these methods are covered for FDA-approved indications and recognized off-label indications listed in drug compendia [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3:3 — 2019-02-01]. Regarding billing responsibility, a policy from July 1, 2020, indicates it is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, such as the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12:8 — 2020-07-01].", "provider_manual_definition": "According to the newest provider manual source dated October 1, 2025, the New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for drugs, supplies, and services associated with home infusion treatments [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01]. The manual also states that NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members via both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]. Drugs are evaluated for pharmacy benefit coverage based on criteria such as being billable in NCPDP format [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01].", "similarities": ["Both the policy documents and the provider manual acknowledge the coverage of drugs administered by intravenous methods [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01], [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:12 — 2025-10-01]."], "differences": ["The provider manual explicitly states that the NYS Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment for home infusion treatments and that drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], whereas the policy documents do not offer specific guidance on bundled payments or the billing mechanism for home infusion drugs.", "The provider manual specifically addresses 'Home Infusion' as a distinct category of treatment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01], while the policy documents discuss drugs administered by intravenous methods more generally without specific reference to 'home infusion' billing or coverage details.", "The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, is more recent and provides more detailed, specific guidance regarding home infusion drug coverage and billing [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:19 — 2025-10-01] compared to the policy documents, with the newest relevant policy effective July 1, 2022 [New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8:4 — 2022-07-01]."], "caveats": null}

The drug must be dispensed by a licensed pharmacy. It implies that the pharmacy is responsible for billing for the drug product itself.
The updates implicitly solidify the rule that a pharmacy cannot bill for the professional nursing component.

test_id_56.question: How is naloxone reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: The provider manual, with an effective date of October 1, 2025, is more recent and provides specific guidance on how naloxone is reimbursed, stating that the pharmacy is reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology. The policy documents, with the newest relevant effective date of August 1, 2021, do not specifically address naloxone reimbursement but discuss general reimbursement for vaccines or services.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents discuss reimbursement for vaccines for members 19 years and older at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — August 1, 2021], and reimbursement for initial authorization of 96 units or 24 hours of service for a 60-day period by MMCPs [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — March 1, 2023].
answers.provider_manual_definition: For naloxone, the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately, with the drug claim using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement method for naloxone, stating it is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025], whereas the policy documents do not specifically mention naloxone reimbursement.

The provider manual specifies that if both the drug and administration are billed, claims must be submitted separately, with the drug claim using the ordering prescriber’s NPI [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide such specific billing instructions for naloxone.
comparison: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement method for naloxone, stating it is reimbursed when billed via NDC using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025], whereas the policy documents do not specifically mention naloxone reimbursement.

The provider manual specifies that if both the drug and administration are billed, claims must be submitted separately, with the drug claim using the ordering prescriber’s NPI [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide such specific billing instructions for naloxone.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provided do not contain specific information regarding the reimbursement of naloxone, focusing instead on general vaccine reimbursement or service authorization. Therefore, the provider manual is the primary source for information on naloxone reimbursement.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

TCN 082609865432123 was paid on

insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.

test_id_56.ground_truth:
"The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.

The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used."
test_id_56.pm_ground_truth: The manual establishes naloxone as a covered drug on the Medicaid formulary, recognizing its use as an opioid antagonist. The guidance is based on a traditional prescription for a specific patient.There are standard co-payment rules for drugs. Under these general rules, a co-payment would apply unless the member is exempt.
test_id_56.policy_ground_truth: The updates dramatically expand this policy beyond individual patients. They introduce and clarify the NYS Naloxone Co-payment Assistance Program (N-CAP) and the concept of dispensing naloxone via a non-patient specific (standing) order to increase community access. For all NYS Medicaid members, including MMC members, there are no co-payments for naloxone.  Effective June 22, 2023, when dispensing via a standing order, the pharmacy must submit its own pharmacy NPI in the prescriber field. • For the Patient: The claim is submitted using the information of the person receiving the naloxone. If that person is a Medicaid member, their own information is used.

answers.headline_summary: The newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, provides specific guidance on how naloxone is reimbursed, including billing for the drug and its administration. In contrast, the newest policy documents, effective March 1, 2023, do not contain sufficient grounded evidence to define how naloxone is reimbursed. Therefore, the provider manual should receive more weight for current guidance.
answers.policy_definition: Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for policy documents. The policy documents discuss reimbursement for vaccines for members 19 years and older at no more than the actual acquisition cost to the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10:10 — August 1, 2021], and reimbursement for initial authorization of 96 units or 24 hours of service for a 60-day period by MMCPs [Children’s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children’s Home and Community Based Services Manual:129 — March 1, 2023]. However, these do not specifically address naloxone.
answers.provider_manual_definition: Naloxone, when billed as a drug, is reimbursed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology, with the claim submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. If billing for the administration of the drug, this claim must be submitted separately using procedure code 96372 and the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. Orders for naloxone must be kept on file at the pharmacy [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025].
differences: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement process for naloxone, including separate billing for the drug and its administration, and the use of NDCs and procedure codes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide any specific information regarding naloxone reimbursement.
comparison: The provider manual explicitly details the reimbursement process for naloxone, including separate billing for the drug and its administration, and the use of NDCs and procedure codes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 1, 2025]. The policy documents do not provide any specific information regarding naloxone reimbursement.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provided do not contain specific information regarding the reimbursement of naloxone, focusing instead on other types of reimbursement such as vaccines or home and community-based services. Therefore, a direct comparison of the definition of naloxone reimbursement is not possible from the policy documents.

answers.provider_manual_definition: According to the newest provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, for naloxone, the pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. If billing for both the drug and its administration, these claims must be submitted separately [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. For the drug, the claim must use the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field, and for administration, procedure code 96372 with the pharmacy’s NPI in the Prescriber field [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025].
differences: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly details the reimbursement process for naloxone, specifying billing via NDC for the drug and separately for administration using procedure code 96372 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents, with the newest relevant effective date of March 1, 2023, do not provide any specific information regarding naloxone reimbursement.
comparison: The provider manual, effective October 1, 2025, explicitly details the reimbursement process for naloxone, specifying billing via NDC for the drug and separately for administration using procedure code 96372 [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines:15 — October 01, 2025]. In contrast, the policy documents, with the newest relevant effective date of March 1, 2023, do not provide any specific information regarding naloxone reimbursement.
answers.caveats: The policy documents provided do not contain specific information regarding the reimbursement of naloxone, focusing instead on other services or medications. Therefore, the provider manual is the sole source of information for this specific concept within the provided context.

provider_manual.text:
These orders must be
kept on file at the pharmacy.
• If billing for both the drug and administration, these claims must be submitted separately.
• For the drug, the claim must be submitted using the ordering prescriber’s NPI in the Prescriber ID field. The
pharmacy will be reimbursed for the medication when billed via National Drug Code (NDC) using
reimbursement methodology.
• For the administration, the claim must be submitted using procedure code 96372, and the pharmacy’s NPI
in the Prescriber ID field. A pharmacy will be reimbursed the current administration fee.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Multiple Source Drugs
Reimbursement is only available for those multiple source drugs contained on the List of Medicaid Reimbursable
Drugs.
For certain brand name prescriptions not in the Brand Less Than Generic Program, to be eligible for reimbursement,
prescribers must certify that the brand name drug is required by:
• writing directly on the face of the prescription “Brand Necessary” or “Brand Medically Necessary” in their own
handwriting in addition to the “DAW”, or
• in the case of electronic prescriptions, the prescriber must insert an electronic direction to clarify “Brand
Medically Necessary” or “Brand Necessary”.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
TCN 082609865432123 was paid on
October 1, 2008. Later, the provider became aware that the patient was covered by other insurance. The other
insurance was billed, and the provider received full payment from that payer. Medicaid must be reimbursed by
submitting a void to the previously paid claim.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
